section.title: Jesus Greater Than Moses
section.sectionRationale: Moses was the towering figure of the old covenant, the servant faithful in all God's house. The writer honours him fully, then shows that Jesus is greater: Moses was a servant within the house, but Christ is the Son over the house, indeed its builder. This prepares the wilderness warning that follows.
section.thesis: Jesus and Moses were both faithful in God's house, but Jesus deserves greater glory, for Moses served as a faithful servant within the house while Christ rules as the faithful Son over it and, as builder, is greater than the house itself; and we are that house if we hold firmly to our hope.
section.frameNote: A tight comparison: the call to consider Jesus as apostle and high priest; the shared faithfulness of Jesus and Moses; the builder greater than the house; and the servant contrasted with the Son over the house, which is us if we hold fast.

units.label: Consider Jesus, apostle and high priest

verses.text: Therefore, holy brothers, who share in the heavenly calling, set your focus on Jesus, the {{apostolos|apostle}} and {{archiereus|high priest}} whom we confess.
He was faithful to the One who appointed Him, just as Moses was faithful in all God’s {{oikos|house}}.

units.label: The builder greater than the house
units.note: God is the builder of everything

verses.text: For Jesus has been counted worthy of greater glory than Moses, just as the builder of a house has greater honor than the house itself.
And every house is built by someone, but God is the builder of everything.

units.label: The servant and the Son over the house
units.note: we are His house, if we hold fast

verses.text: Now Moses was faithful as a servant in all God’s house, testifying to what would be spoken later.
But Christ is faithful as the Son over God’s house. And we are His house, if we hold firmly to our confidence and the hope of which we boast.

apostolos.gloss: One sent with authority as a representative, an envoy. Uniquely in the New Testament, Jesus is called the apostle, the one God sent to speak and act for Him, greater than Moses, whom God also sent to Pharaoh and to Israel.

archiereus.gloss: High priest, the chief mediator who represents the people before God. The letter's great theme: Jesus is the high priest we confess, combining in Himself the roles that were divided between Moses and Aaron under the old covenant.

oikos.gloss: A house or household, both the building and the family within it. God's house is His people; Moses served faithfully within it, but Christ, as builder and Son, rules over it, and believers are that house if they hold fast.

basic.context: Moses was the greatest figure of the Old Testament for Jewish readers, the one who led the exodus and received the law. The writer does not run Moses down; he calls him faithful in all God's house. But he shows Jesus is greater still. Moses was a servant in the house; Jesus is the Son over it and, as its builder, greater than the house itself.
basic.hermeneutics: The comparison draws on Numbers 12, where God calls Moses faithful in all His house. Read the argument through the images: servant versus Son, house versus builder. The conclusion is a call to hold fast, since we are that house.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus is uniquely called the apostolos, the sent one, and the archiereus, high priest, of our confession. The recurring word is oikos, house or household: Moses served in the house, Christ is Son over the house, and we are that house.
basic.typology: Moses, the faithful servant and mediator, foreshadows Christ, the faithful Son and greater mediator. Moses' testimony pointed forward to what would be spoken later, fulfilled in the Son.
basic.christ: Christ is the apostle and high priest we confess, faithful like Moses but greater, for He is the Son over God's house and its very builder, sharing in the honour of God who builds all things.
basic.god: God appointed both Moses and the Son, and God is the builder of everything. He is faithful to His purpose, raising up servants and finally His Son to rule over His household.
basic.crossReferences: Numbers 12 calls Moses faithful in God's house. John 5 records Jesus saying Moses wrote of Him. 1 Peter 2 describes believers as a spiritual house.
basic.oneStory: God has always been building a house, a people for Himself. Moses served within it; the Son now rules over it as builder and heir, and all who hold fast belong to it.

context.literary: The passage opens with 'therefore', drawing on the merciful high priest of chapter 2, and introduces the twin titles apostle and high priest. It builds a comparison in three steps and ends with a conditional clause, 'if we hold firmly', that launches the warning of 3:7-19.
context.historical: For readers tempted to return to Moses and the temple system, the writer reframes Moses' greatness: he was faithful precisely as a servant pointing forward, and the one he pointed to has now come.
context.canonical: The passage develops the biblical image of God's house or temple as His people, links Moses' mediation to Christ's, and undergirds the New Testament theme that Scripture, including Moses, testifies to Christ.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To exalt Christ above Moses without dishonouring Moses, and to press the readers, as members of God's house, to hold firmly to their confidence and hope in Christ.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The passage describes the relative glory of Moses and Christ and then turns prescriptive in verse 6: we are God's house if we hold fast, a call to persevering faith.

debates.question: Does 'if we hold firmly' make our membership in God's house uncertain?

views.label: Perseverance is the mark of genuine belonging
views.case: Those who truly belong to Christ hold fast; the condition describes the evidence of real faith, and God preserves His own so that they do endure to the end.

views.label: A genuine warning that must be heeded
views.case: The 'if' is a real summons to continue; presuming on belonging while abandoning hope is precisely the danger, and the warning is a means God uses to keep His people.

debates.bearing: Either way the exhortation stands: keep holding to your confidence and hope, for endurance marks those who are truly Christ's house.

study.originalLanguages: The writer gives Jesus two titles that sum up the letter: apostolos, the authoritative sent one who represents God to us, and archiereus, the high priest who represents us to God. The controlling image is oikos, which means both a building and the family within it, God's household. Moses was faithful therapōn, as a servant, within that house; Christ is faithful as huios, Son, over it, and as its builder He holds greater honour than the house itself. Because we are that house, the passage turns to exhortation: hold firmly to the confidence and hope we profess.

typology.type: Moses, faithful servant in God's house
typology.fulfillment: A true type of faithfulness, surpassed by Christ the faithful Son over the house.

typology.type: Moses' testimony to what would be spoken later
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the Son, the later and final word to which Moses pointed.

typology.type: The tabernacle and household of Israel
typology.fulfillment: Fulfilled in the church, God's house built by the Son, indwelt by God among His people.

study.christ: Christ is the apostle and high priest of our confession, the one whom we are called to consider and fix our focus upon. He was faithful like Moses, but He surpasses Moses as the builder surpasses the house and as a son surpasses a servant. Moses served within God's household and testified to what was still to come; Christ rules over that household as the Son and is Himself, with God, its builder. In Him the roles of sent envoy and mediating priest are joined, and the people He rules are His own house.
study.god: God is the one who appointed the Son and who is the builder of everything. His faithfulness runs through the whole household of His people, from the servant Moses to the Son who reigns. God's purpose has always been to dwell with a people He builds for Himself, and He brings that purpose to its height in the Son over the house. The call to hold fast rests on the faithfulness of this God, who keeps the house He is building.

crossReferences.note: Moses faithful in all My house, the text the argument builds upon.
Moses wrote of Me, showing his testimony pointed forward to Christ.
Believers as living stones built into a spiritual house, matching we are His house.
We share in Christ if we hold firmly to the end, echoing the condition of verse 6.

study.oneStory: Throughout Scripture God is building a house, a people among whom He dwells. Moses served faithfully within that house and pointed ahead to something greater. Now the Son has come as builder and heir, ruling over God's household in glory greater than Moses ever had. The whole story leads to this house, and the invitation is to belong to it by holding fast, with confidence and hope, to the faithful Son.

quiz.q: How does the writer relate Jesus to Moses?

options.text: Both were faithful, but Jesus is greater, as the Son and builder over the house.
options.note: Verses 5 to 6 contrast the faithful servant with the faithful Son over the house.

options.text: Moses was a fraud and Jesus exposed him.
options.note: The writer honours Moses as truly faithful; the point is Jesus is greater still.

options.text: Jesus and Moses are exactly equal.
options.note: Jesus is counted worthy of greater glory than Moses.

options.text: Moses was greater because he came first.
options.note: Coming first does not make Moses greater; the Son surpasses the servant.

quiz.q: What is the difference between Moses' role and Christ's in God's house?

options.text: Moses was a servant in the house; Christ is the Son over the house.
options.note: Verses 5 to 6 draw exactly this distinction.

options.text: Both were merely guests in the house.
options.note: Moses served in it and Christ rules over it; neither is a mere guest.

options.text: Moses built the house and Christ only lived in it.
options.note: The reverse: Christ is the builder, greater than the house.

options.text: There is no difference in their roles.
options.note: The roles differ sharply: servant within versus Son over.

quiz.q: What are the two titles given to Jesus in verse 1?

options.note: He is the sent one and the mediating priest of our confession.
Those roles are true elsewhere, but verse 1 names apostle and high priest.
The titles are apostle and high priest, not servant and scribe.
He teaches and fulfils the law, but the verse names apostle and high priest.

quiz.q: What does verse 6 say marks those who are God's house?

options.text: Holding firmly to our confidence and the hope of which we boast.
options.note: Perseverance in hope marks genuine belonging.
It is holding to hope, not flawless performance, that is called for.
Belonging is by holding fast to Christ, not by ancestry.

options.text: A one-time decision with no continuing.
options.note: The emphasis falls on continuing to hold firmly.

questions: The writer urges us to fix our focus on Jesus. What competes for that focus in your daily attention?
Jesus is both the apostle sent from God and the high priest who represents us to God. How does holding both together enrich the way you relate to Him?
We are God's house if we hold firmly to our hope. What helps you keep holding on when your confidence feels thin?
Moses was faithful, yet only pointed forward to Christ. Are there good things or people you have let overshadow the greater Son?
Christ rules as Son over the household you belong to. How does living as part of His house shape your commitment to His people?

translationNotes.note: Jesus is called 'the apostle', the only place in the New Testament this title is given to Him; it names Him the authoritative one sent by God, paired with high priest.
The wording echoes Numbers 12:7, where God commends Moses as faithful in all His house; the writer takes up this praise to set the comparison.
Some manuscripts add 'to the end' to 'hold firmly', matching the similar phrase in verse 14 and underscoring perseverance.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for Hebrews 3:1-6. No em dashes occurred in this passage. Sermon set to none; no expositor series confirmed this session.
